name: Theodora
meaning: From Greek Theodōra, a compound of theos meaning 'god' and dōron meaning 'gift'. Literally 'gift of God', borne by Byzantine empresses and Christian saints.
longevity_read: Imperial and beautiful; ages with Byzantine grandeur and surges in English-speaking markets.
trend_context: Theodora has been gaining ground steadily since the 2010s, with sharp uptick in the last decade as parents revisit Byzantine and Greek-classical names.

name: Olivia
meaning: From the Latin oliva meaning 'olive tree'. Popularised by Shakespeare in Twelfth Night; the olive branch carries connotations of peace and abundance.
longevity_read: Quietly dominant since the early 2000s; sits at or near the top of name charts year after year without feeling overused.
trend_context: Olivia hit US #1 in 2019 and has held in the top three since. Stable, not fading.

name: Theo
meaning: Short form of Theodore (from Greek Theodōros, 'gift of God'). Stands alone as a complete given name across most of Europe.
longevity_read: A nickname that grew up: Theo cleared the threshold from diminutive to standalone in the 2010s and now leads in UK and French charts.
trend_context: Theo's UK rank climbed from outside the top 100 in 2010 to inside the top 20 in 2024. Same pattern in France since 2018.

name: Bartholomew
meaning: From the Aramaic Bar-Tolmai, 'son of Tolmai'. Borne by one of the twelve apostles in the New Testament.
longevity_read: A name that wears its centuries openly. Bartholomew never trends but never disappears — held mostly by older men and the occasional contrarian newborn.
trend_context: Bartholomew has been on a slow decline since the 1970s; modern parents who pick it are making a deliberate counter-cultural choice.

name: Zoë
meaning: Direct translation of the Hebrew name Eve (Chava) into Greek: zōē meaning 'life'. The diaeresis on the ë marks two separate syllables, preserving the original Greek pronunciation.
longevity_read: Crisp, international, and instantly recognisable. Zoë sits in the top 30 across most Western European countries with remarkable consistency.
trend_context: Zoë (with and without diacritics) has held a top-50 position in the US, UK, Netherlands and France for two decades — a rare flat trend at high volume.

name: Penelope
meaning: From Greek Pēnelópē, possibly from pēnē meaning 'weft' (of a loom). Famously the wife of Odysseus in Homer's Odyssey, who wove and unwove a tapestry to delay her suitors during his twenty-year absence.
longevity_read: A Homeric name with serious literary weight that nonetheless feels fresh in the 2020s. Penelope returned to the top 25 in the US around 2015 and has stayed there since.
trend_context: After half a century of decline (peak 1900s, low 1970s-90s), Penelope has more than tripled in US births since 2010 — a textbook revival.

name: Aleksandr
meaning: Russian and Slavic form of Alexander, from Greek Aléxandros: aléxō meaning 'to defend' + anēr meaning 'man'. 'Defender of men.'
longevity_read: Aleksandr carries the weight of tsars and writers without feeling antiquated. Sasha as a nickname unlocks it for a softer everyday register.
trend_context: Aleksandr (and variant spellings) has held top-20 positions across Russia and Slavic Europe for over a century. Stability is its identity.

name: Cassia
meaning: From the Latin Cassia, from the Greek kasía — a fragrant flowering plant in the cinnamon family. A botanical name with quiet ancient resonance.
longevity_read: Cassia is the kind of name that feels found rather than chosen — botanical, unforced, with just enough Latin texture to feel deliberate.
trend_context: Outside US top 1000 until 2015; entered the top 800 by 2020 and continues climbing. Tracks the broader botanical-revival trend (Hazel, Magnolia, Juniper).
